mnemonics.title: POPULATION vs SAMPLE
mnemonics.content: PAPA: Population All Parameter, Sample Any Statistic - Population parameters (μ, σ) describe POPULATION; Sample statistics (x̄, s) describe SAMPLE

mnemonics.title: Statistical Inference Types
mnemonics.content: TWO PI: TWO types of inference are - Point/Interval estimation and Hypothesis testing - PI helps remember both Estimation and Hypothesis

mnemonics.title: Key Terms Memory
mnemonics.content: PEARL: Population (entire), Estimate (from sample), Attribute (characteristic), Random (equal chance), Large data (use inference)

acronyms.usage: States sampling distribution approaches normal as sample size increases, enabling inference
Range of values within which the population parameter likely falls
The default assumption that there is no effect or relationship in the population
The claim we want to test against the null hypothesis
The standard deviation of the sampling distribution of a statistic

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a large jar of mixed colored candies. Population = ALL candies in jar. Sample = handful of candies drawn. We use the handful to estimate colors in entire jar.
Think of taking many samples (each of size n), calculating mean for each, then plotting these means. This distribution clusters around the true population mean.
Parameter = the true answer hidden in the vault (population). Statistic = the key (sample) we use to estimate what's in the vault.
Imagine shooting arrows at a target. The bullseye is the true parameter. A 95% CI means if we shoot 100 arrows, 95 will hit near the bullseye.

analogies.analogy: Like a detective solving a case. The population is the complete crime scene (too large to examine fully). The sample is the evidence collected. Using this evidence, the detective (inference) draws conclusions about what happened.
Like tasting one spoonful of soup to judge the entire pot. The spoonful (sample) gives you a good idea of how the whole pot (population) tastes.
Like drawing names from a hat where every name has an equal chance. This fairness ensures the sample represents everyone fairly.
Like measuring height with a slightly stretched tape measure. The error is natural and expected - not a mistake but inherent in the process of estimation.
